file_name: Anbalagan and Malathi - 2024 - Horse herd optimization with gate recurrent unit for an automatic classification of different facial.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning-based Gate Recurrent Unit (GRU) for the automatic classification of facial skin diseases (such as acne, eczema, melanoma, and basal cell carcinoma). The method also incorporates meta-heuristic optimization (Horse Herd Optimization) for feature selection and image processing techniques (non-linear diffusion, adaptive histogram equalization) for pre-processing.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a new deep learning model (Horse Herd Optimization with Gate Recurrent Unit) for classifying facial skin diseases. It details a specific methodology including image pre-processing, feature extraction, and classification, and reports original results (98.2% accuracy) obtained from experiments on the Kaggle dataset, comparing them against existing classifiers.

title: Horse Herd Optimization with Gate Recurrent Unit for an Automatic Classification of Different Facial Skin Disease
journal: Journal of Imaging Informatics in Medicine
brief_summary: This study proposes a deep learning-based system for the automatic classification of facial skin diseases, including acne, eczema, melanoma, and basal cell carcinoma. The methodology employs non-linear diffusion, adaptive histogram equalization, and high boost filtering for image pre-processing, followed by feature extraction using GLRM and CCoLBP. Horse Herd Optimization (HOA) is utilized for meta-heuristic feature selection to enhance classification accuracy. The model was evaluated on a Kaggle dataset containing images of rosacea, eczema, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, and acne. The proposed GRU-based classifier achieved a 98.2% accuracy, 97.1% precision, and 95.5% F1-score. The results indicate that the proposed technique outperforms existing methods in detecting facial skin conditions.

all_task_types.target: Facial skin disease categories (acne, actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, eczema, rosacea)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs automatic classification of different facial skin diseases using a Gate Recurrent Unit (GRU) classifier. The task is multiclass classification with 5 distinct classes (Class 0: acne, Class 1: actinic keratosis, Class 2: basal cell carcinoma, Class 3: eczema, Class 4: rosacea) based on the Kaggle dataset. Performance is evaluated using accuracy (98.2%), precision (97.1%), F1-score (95.5%), and confusion matrices.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Face Skin Disease Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset hosted on Kaggle (referenced as Dataset 1 in the paper, URL: https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/amellia/face-skin-disease) containing images of facial skin diseases.

use.training: The dataset was split into training and testing sets for model development.
use.testing: The dataset was split into training and testing sets for model evaluation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance metrics (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, error, FDR, NPV) of the proposed GRU classifier across different data folds to ensure robustness.

To calculate and compare performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, error, F1-score, kappa, MCC, NPV, FPR, FNR, FDR, and FOR against existing classifiers (RBFNN, DBN, ANN, RF).
To visualize and compare the diagnostic ability of the proposed GRU classifier against existing methods.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the proposed GRU model and existing classifiers (RBFNN, DBN, ANN, RF) were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, F1-score, etc.) without statistical significance testing.

Kaggle Face Skin Disease Dataset.metric: Accuracy, Precision, Error, Specificity, F1-score, Sensitivity, Kappa, FPR, FNR, MCC, NPV, FOR
Kaggle Face Skin Disease Dataset.notes: The proposed GRU model achieved the highest performance across all metrics compared to CNN, DCNN, KNN, RBFNN, DBN, ANN, and RF. The dataset consisted of images for acne, actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, eczema, and rosacea. The model was trained for 100 epochs with a batch size of 200, initial learning rate of 0.005, and dropout of 0.2. The execution time was reported as 11.2 seconds.

K-Fold Cross-Validation (10 Folds).metric: Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity, Error, FDR, NPV
K-Fold Cross-Validation (10 Folds).notes: 10-fold cross-validation was performed. The paper also mentions a 'Fourfold Cross-Validation' section with specific train/test splits (Fold 1: 60-40, Fold 2: 70-30, Fold 3: 90-10, Fold 4: 80-20), but Table 5 explicitly lists 10 folds. The results in Table 5 correspond to the 10-fold evaluation.

architecture_used.input_size: 256x256 (optimal size for accuracy; resizing applied to input images)

architecture_details.layers: Three layers: Input layer (10 nodes), Hidden layer, Output layer (2 nodes)
architecture_details.activation_functions: Sigmoid (σ) for gates, Tanh for candidate output
architecture_details.feature_input: Features extracted via Grey Level Run-length Matrix (GLRM) and Chromatic Co-occurrence Local Binary Pattern (CCoLBP), selected via Horse Herd Optimization (HOA)

training_details.optimizer: Adam (implied by 'Gradient moving' and 'Initial learn rate' parameters typical of Adam, though not explicitly named)
training_details.loss_function: Mean Square Error (used for HOA fitness evaluation; specific GRU loss not explicitly named but standard cross-entropy is implied for classification)
training_details.hardware: Intel Core i5, Nvidia GeForce GTX 1650, 16 GB RAM

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing (to alter image dimensions and reduce file size)
Grey Scale Conversion (removing color information to leave shades of grey)
Non-linear Diffusion (used for noise removal/denoising while preserving edges)
Adaptive Histogram Equalization (AHE) (used for contrast enhancement/luminosity improvement)
High Boost Filtering (HBF) (used for image sharpening/edge enhancement)
conclusions: The proposed Horse Herd Optimization with Gate Recurrent Unit (HOA-GRU) model achieved a classification accuracy of 98.2%, which outperformed existing classifiers including CNN (92.9%), DCNN (88%), KNN (80%), RBFNN (92%), DBN (88.2%), ANN (83.5%), and RF (76.2%).
The model demonstrated high precision (97.1%), F1-score (95.5%), and specificity (95.9%), with a low error rate of 1.8%.

The integration of meta-heuristic optimization (Horse Herd Optimization) for feature selection and deep learning (GRU) for classification effectively addressed issues of deficient disease detection at early stages and insufficient image enhancement found in previous methods.

The proposed technique is considered the best choice for classifying various facial skin conditions (acne, actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, eczema, and rosacea) compared to current techniques.

The study acknowledges that existing medical diagnosis methods are often time-consuming and inaccurate for early detection, and that advanced image processing tools are necessary to improve diagnostic quality and reduce reliance on costly medical equipment and expertise.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on a single public Kaggle dataset, which limits the assessment of generalizability and introduces potential dataset bias; no external validation on independent clinical cohorts was performed.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for performance comparisons against baseline models, relying instead on descriptive metrics.

There is a discrepancy in the reported cross-validation methodology: the text describes a 'Fourfold Cross-Validation' with varying train/test splits (60-40, 70-30, 90-10, 80-20), while Table 5 presents results for 10 folds, creating ambiguity about the primary validation protocol.

The dataset composition is not fully transparent; the paper lists 'acnitic keratosis' (likely a typo for actinic keratosis) and does not specify the number of images per class or the total dataset size, hindering reproducibility.

The proposed model uses hand-crafted features (GLRM and CCoLBP) combined with meta-heuristic optimization (HOA) rather than end-to-end deep learning, which may limit its ability to capture complex hierarchical representations compared to modern CNNs.

The hardware specifications (Intel Core i5, Nvidia GeForce GTX 1650) and software environment (Python 3.8) are reported, but the specific version of the Kaggle dataset used is not cited with a version number or access date, risking data leakage or reproducibility issues if the dataset changes.

The claim of 'best choice' for classification is based solely on superior accuracy (98.2%) compared to a limited set of baselines (CNN, DCNN, KNN, RBFNN, DBN, ANN, RF) on one dataset, without comparison to state-of-the-art transfer learning models or recent architectures.
